input: Abductive reasoning: A Phase 3 trial of a novel antibiotic shows superiority for clinical cure rate but HIGHER mortality than comparator. Generate ranked explanations.

expected: antibiotic effective for infection but has off-target organ toxicity
clinical cure = microbiologic success but doesn't capture systemic harm
imbalance in baseline severity between arms despite randomization
drug-drug interaction with common ICU co-medications
delayed toxicity not captured by clinical cure endpoint

input: Abductive reasoning: Higher expression of a 'tumor suppressor' gene correlates with WORSE prognosis in breast cancer. Explain.

expected: gene has context-dependent oncogenic function in breast
expression is a marker of aggressive subtype (not causal)
compensatory upregulation in response to other driver mutations
measurement artifact (gene has multiple isoforms with different functions)

input: Abductive reasoning: A drug designed to inhibit Target A shows clinical efficacy, but genetic knockout of Target A in mice has no effect on the disease. Explain.

expected: drug has off-target activity on the actual therapeutic target
pharmacologic inhibition ≠ genetic ablation (incomplete inhibition may be therapeutic)
mouse model doesn't recapitulate human disease biology
drug acts through a metabolite, not the parent compound

input: Abductive reasoning: Gastric cancer incidence is 5x higher in Japan/Korea than in the US. Generate explanations.

expected: H. pylori strain virulence differences (cagA+ strains)
dietary factors (high salt, preserved foods)
screening bias (earlier detection in J/K inflates incidence)
genetic susceptibility (HLA polymorphisms)
environmental carcinogens

input: Abductive reasoning: 30% of patients have complete response to Drug X while 70% have zero response. No known biomarker distinguishes them. Explain the bimodal distribution.

expected: undiscovered pharmacogenomic variant affecting drug metabolism
tumor subtype heterogeneity not captured by current profiling
immune microenvironment determines response (T-cell inflamed vs desert)
drug exposure variability (absorption differences creating bimodal PK)

input: Abductive reasoning: Drug cured 90% of mice with Alzheimer's-like pathology but failed Phase 3 in humans. Why?

expected: mouse model doesn't recapitulate human AD complexity
timing: mice treated before pathology, humans treated after neurodegeneration
species-specific amyloid biology
dose scaling issues
human AD is heterogeneous, mouse model is single-mechanism

input: Abductive reasoning: A serious adverse event cluster (3 cases of liver failure) occurs at one clinical trial site but not the other 15 sites. Explain.

expected: site-specific concomitant medication or herbal supplement use
drug storage/handling error at that site (temperature excursion)
patient population difference (e.g., hepatitis B endemic area)
coincidence (3 cases can cluster by chance in small N)
site-specific genotypic enrichment (HLA allele)

input: Abductive reasoning: A pain trial shows 45% placebo response rate, obscuring drug effect. Why is placebo response so high?

expected: regression to mean (enrolled during pain flare)
expectation effects (trial participation increases hope)
natural disease fluctuation
Hawthorne effect (increased attention/care)
measurement instrument sensitivity (VAS subjective)

input: Abductive reasoning: A cancer patient responds completely to a targeted therapy but relapses within 6 weeks. Explain the unusually rapid resistance.

expected: pre-existing resistant subclone (not de novo mutation)
drug-tolerant persister cells survived and expanded
epigenetic resistance (rapid, doesn't require mutation)
incomplete target inhibition at steady-state concentrations
bypass pathway was already active at low level

input: Abductive reasoning: Countries with higher chocolate consumption per capita have more Nobel laureates per capita. Generate explanations ranking correlation vs causation.

expected: confounding by national wealth (rich countries buy more chocolate AND fund more research)
confounding by education system quality
confounding by climate/cultural factors
ecological fallacy (country-level data doesn't apply to individuals)
pure coincidence with small N of countries
